## The Numbers Grand Hotel $\text{[hotel:1.0em]{.twemoji}}$

:::{.fun-fact image="/static_images/hotel.pdf" width="0.7" scale="1.0"}
I am probably the first person to use the analogy of a Grand Hotel to explain mathematical concepts $\text{[wink:1.5em]{.twemoji}}$.

Our Grand Hotel is a special one with ten rooms on each floor. The owner is a reasonable man, which is why the floor and room numbers start at zero.
:::


Our hotel rooms look like this. They have  a door and a number on top of it.

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | fig-align: "center"
# | fig-cap: "Room number $7$."

from yoyo_plots.hotel import Room, Number
from yoyo_plots.common import display_vector

room = Room(0, 0, width=150, height=200, values=[Number(7, "black")])
display_vector(room)
```

The numbers on top of the door are very useful to identify the room. If our hotel has only one floor, we can easily identify a particular room with a single digit number.

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | fig-align: "center"
# | fig-cap: "Ground floor (floor $0$) of our Grand Hotel."

from yoyo_plots.hotel import Floor
from yoyo_plots.common import display_vector

floor = Floor(0, 0, 1)
display_vector(floor)
```

You wouldn't have any problem finding room number $7$ in the first floor, right? But what happen if our hotel has $2$ or more floors? 




```{python}
# | echo: false
# | fig-align: "center"
# | fig-cap: "The first two floors of our Grand Hotel. Rooms have single-digit numbers."

from yoyo_plots.hotel import Building
from yoyo_plots.common import display_vector

building = Building(0, 0, 1, 2, without_offset=True, pad_zeros=False, roof=True)
display_vector(building)
```

You cannot unambiguously identify Room $7$ using single-digit numbers. Clearly, we need more numbers to represent additional rooms, and you already know how to construct larger numbers.  As we saw in the previous section, the first thing we need to do to represent larger numbers is to add more positions.


```{python}
# | echo: false
# | fig-align: "center"
# | fig-cap: "The first two floors of our Grand Hotel. Each room number has now two placeholders (two boxes)."

from yoyo_plots.hotel import Building
from yoyo_plots.common import display_vector

building = Building(0, 0, 2, 2, without_offset=True, pad_zeros=False, roof=True)
display_vector(building)
```

:::{.question}
Adding one position is not enough; we also need to fill them with digits. Which digits should we use to unambiguously identify a particular room?
:::

That's right; you also need to specify the floor number. For example, you could say "Room $7$ on the $0$ floor" or "Room $7$ on the $1$ floor."

We will now add a second digit to each door in the second placeholder to represent the floor number. The first position, the rightmost one, is for the room number; the second position is for the $\color{red}{\text{floor number}}$.


```{python}
# | echo: false
# | fig-align: "center"
# | fig-cap: "First $2$ floors (floor $0$ and floor $1$) of our Grand Hotel."

from yoyo_plots.hotel import Building
from yoyo_plots.common import display_vector

building = Building(0, 0, 2, 2, pad_zeros=True, roof=True)
display_vector(building)
```

In the first floor, we don't really need those padding zeros in front, so we can safely remove them. The following numbering is equivalent to the previous one.


```{python}
# | echo: false
# | fig-align: "center"
# | fig-cap: "First $2$ floors (floor $0$ and floor $1$) of our Grand Hotel."

from yoyo_plots.hotel import Building
from yoyo_plots.common import display_vector

building = Building(0, 0, 2, 2, pad_zeros=False, roof=True)
display_vector(building)
```

To get from one room on the ground floor (floor $0$) to the corresponding room on the first floor (floor $1$), we have to climb a ladder.


```{python}
# | echo: false
# | fig-align: "center"
# | fig-cap: "First $2$ floors (floor $0$ and floor $1$) of our Grand Hotel with ladder."

from yoyo_plots.hotel import Building
from yoyo_plots.common import display_vector

building = Building(0, 0, 2, 2, pad_zeros=False, roof=True)

for i in range(9):
    building.add_ladder(0, i, "red")
display_vector(building)
```

Climbing a ladder means adding $10$. For example, climbing a ladder from Room $7$ on the ground floor will take you to Room $17$ on the first floor, which is $17 = 7 + 10$. 


```{python}
# | echo: false
# | fig-align: "center"
# | fig-cap: "Climbing a ladder means adding $10$."

from yoyo_plots.hotel import Ladder, Label, Number
from yoyo_plots.common import display_vector

ladder = Ladder(x=0, y=0, width=60, height=200, color="red")
label1 = Label([None, Number(7, "black")])
label2 = Label([Number(1, "red"), Number(7, "black")])

svg = ladder.to_svg(top_label=label2, bottom_label=label1, annotation="+10")
display_vector(svg)
```


If we need to go further, we have to climb more ladders. Think of the second placeholder in each room (the red digit) as the floor number or the number of ladders climbed from the ground floor to get there.

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | fig-align: "center"
# | fig-cap: "The first $5$ floors of our Grand Hotel."

from yoyo_plots.hotel import Building
from yoyo_plots.common import display_vector

n = 5
building = Building(0, 0, 2, n, without_offset=False, pad_zeros=False, roof=True, plants=True, plant_scale=2.5)
for i in range(n-1):
    building.add_ladder(i, 3, "red")

display_vector(building)
```

Ladders can be stacked. Every ladder represents the number of times we add $10$.

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | fig-align: "center"
# | fig-cap: "Climbing three ladders means adding $3 \\times 10=10 + 10 + 10 = 30$."

from yoyo_plots.hotel import Ladder, Label, Number, draw_ladders
from yoyo_plots.common import display_vector

ladder1 = Ladder(x=0, y=0, width=60, height=200, color="red")
ladder2 = Ladder(x=0, y=0, width=60, height=200, color="red")
ladder3 = Ladder(x=0, y=0, width=60, height=200, color="red")

label1 = Label([None, Number(7, "black")])
label2 = Label([Number(3, "red"), Number(7, "black")])

svg = draw_ladders(
    [(ladder1, "+10"), (ladder2, "+10"), (ladder3, "+10")],  # bottom first
    top_label=label2,
    bottom_label=label1,
)
display_vector(svg)
```


### Exercises {.unlisted .unnumbered}

Fill in the blanks with the numbers of the missing rooms.

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | fig-align: "center"
# | fig-cap: "The first $5$ floors of our Grand Hotel."

from yoyo_plots.hotel import Building
from yoyo_plots.common import display_vector

skip_rooms = list(range(15, 25)) + list(range(50, 71)) + [29, 30, 47, 48, 49]

n = 8
building = Building(0, 0, 2, n, without_offset=False, pad_zeros=False, roof=True, plants=True, plant_scale=2.5, skip_rooms=skip_rooms)
for i in range(n-1):
    building.add_ladder(i, 3, "red")

display_vector(building)
```


You saw that adding $10$ is quite easy, you just have to climb one ladder to the next floor.


:::{.font-size-adjust size="25pt"}
$$
\begin{aligned}
0 + 10 &= 10\\
1 + 10 &= 11\\
2 + 10 &= \boxed{\phantom{13}}\\
3 + 10 &= \boxed{\phantom{13}}\\
4 + 10 &= \boxed{\phantom{13}}\\
5 + 10 &= \boxed{\phantom{13}}\\
6 + 10 &= \boxed{\phantom{13}}\\
7 + 10 &= \boxed{\phantom{13}}\\
8 + 10 &= \boxed{\phantom{13}}\\
9 + 10 &= \boxed{\phantom{13}}\\
1 + 20 &= 21\\
7 + 20 &= \boxed{\phantom{13}}\\
2 + 30 &= \boxed{\phantom{13}}\\
2 + 40 &= \boxed{\phantom{13}}\\
\end{aligned}
$$
:::
